author: @RePeteAndMe
text:
Sure. But what about my Cyclonic Stirling engine? Instead of a displacer piston it has a displacer rotor in the cylinder head which spins. Said rotor covers 2/3 of the cylinder head's fins (and displaces as close to 2/3 of its gas as possible) at any one time. As the rotor spins it uncovers hot then Regen then cold then Regen then hot again fins.
Strange how nobody cares about my solutions. I'm confident in my self-discrimination; I'm always way critical of my conclusions. But I can't fathom why nobody will ever engage with me.

text: Only "issue" is that the maximum rpm is set by gravity pulling down the displacer, to make this any faster would require alternating the magnet north south, either by rotating the single magnet, or by having a disk magnets alternating north south

author: @pokeboybob1
text: have you ever considered making a “water brake” or “Joule Machine” to collect heat directly from a windmill rather than creating a electric generator? This would be the opposite of converting heat difference into mechanical movement and would directly convert mechanical movement into heat. Aparently this was popular in Holand in the 70s when there was also fuel shortages.

replies.text:
I too would enjoy just such a video. I think my son is even getting some things together out of the shop to see if we can cobble something together along with you in the future. Of course we’ll either have to order the working bit, or make plans and have a shop cnc larger wheels for the Sterling. If he can manage the storage, I think I’d like to make a rather large version and actually put it to work. Now that’s some inspiration. Good on you, sir. I’ll have to jump over to your support page.  
God Bless, From the States!

author: @aadhithvarathan5432
text:
What is the efficiency of this new Stirling Engine? As of what I know, theoretical max of any Stirling Engine is 40% but typical Gamma Stirlings (the one in the video) is about 9%.
May I know how closer to 40% the magnet moves to?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: just the fact it will run of  smaller heat difference indicates a higher efficiency - but I haven't measured it

replies.author: @aadhithvarathan5432
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Great. Hope to see a number soon.

author: @peoplesresearchcenter
text: I may be wrong, but technically I don't think that type of engine (with a magnetic displacer actuator) can be properly called "Ringbom" which uses internal pressure differentials. A standard Ringbom would not have an issue as far as Curie temperature. (Heat can sometimes weaken a magnet), not an issue for an LTD but something to be aware of.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: technically you are quite right - in an engine of this kind with neodymium magnets you are never going to reach the curie point

author: @ScarletFlames1
text: Wait, so the problem with using a heat punp ambient energy harvester can be circumvented with this as it operates at high efficiency with a 10 degree difference which is 20 times more than the minimum needed for this to work. Upscale it/make lots of these and sandwich them between the hot and cold sides with the cold side up top to harvest ambient heat+sunlight which will get added into the system as harvestable energy via allowing the hot side to reach higher temperatures.

@ThinkingandTinkering I'm actually planning on drawing up a plan with my brother who has CAD experience and getting financial support from a rich uncle (cliche, I know, but if it works don't knock it is what I say!) to build it once I have it all down. 

Thanks for the inspiration btw, I got it all from you! If this plan ever succeeds you can definitely expect to be credited (if it fails I will bear the 'shame' by myself)
